---
title: "Ch 11 — Direct and Inverse Proportions"
subtitle: "When two quantities march in step, and when they trade places"
lesson-id: "cbse8.direct-inverse-proportions"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 11
ncert-chapter-title: "Direct and Inverse Proportions"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.ratio-proportion"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.linear-equations-two-var", "cbse10.applications-trigonometry"]
applied-domains: ["databases", "ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute "$3$ for $\text{₹}60$, so $5$ for ?". If not,
   revisit
   **[Ratio and Proportion](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)**.
2. Are you comfortable with $\tfrac{a}{b} = \tfrac{c}{d}$ as the
   definition of two ratios being equal?
3. If $4$ workers take $3$ days, do you have a *guess* (without
   computing) whether $8$ workers take more or fewer days?
4. Can you compute $\tfrac{60 \times 5}{3}$ in your head?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Open your kitchen and find a recipe. "Tea for 4 people: 800 ml water."
You're hosting 6 people instead.

The widget below scales the recipe.

```{ojs}
viewof people = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "People"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const baseP = 4;
  const baseW = 800; // ml water
  const baseT = 8;   // g tea leaves
  const baseM = 200; // ml milk
  const factor = people / baseP;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.6;">
    <table style="border-collapse:collapse;">
      <tr><th style="text-align:left;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">Ingredient</th><th style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">For 4</th><th style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">For ${people}</th></tr>
      <tr><td style="padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">Water</td><td style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">${baseW} ml</td><td style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">${(baseW*factor).toFixed(0)} ml</td></tr>
      <tr><td style="padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">Tea leaves</td><td style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">${baseT} g</td><td style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">${(baseT*factor).toFixed(1)} g</td></tr>
      <tr><td style="padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">Milk</td><td style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">${baseM} ml</td><td style="text-align:right;padding:0.2rem 0.6rem;">${(baseM*factor).toFixed(0)} ml</td></tr>
    </table>
  </div>`;
}
```

Slide the people count from $4$ to $12$. Every ingredient triples
together. They march in step. Now imagine a *different* problem: you
have $24$ litres of paint, enough to paint one wall in $3$ days. With
twice the painters, half the time. Two quantities, but moving in
*opposite* directions.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. In the tea recipe, doubling the people doubles every ingredient.
   What stays *the same*? Look at "ml of water per person", not just
   "ml of water".
2. With more painters, fewer days. Is "people × days" constant?
3. If you draw "ingredient amount" on the vertical axis against
   "people" on the horizontal, what shape do you get? What if you
   plotted "days" against "painters"?
4. Can you imagine a pair of quantities that go neither *both up* nor
   *one up, one down*?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

The recipe quantities have a constant *ratio* per person — that is
**direct proportion**. The painters and the days have a constant
*product* — that is **inverse proportion**. The chapter is built on
spotting which of the two regimes a problem belongs to.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Recipe scaling

If $5$ kg of rice costs $\text{₹}350$, how much do $8$ kg cost?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set up the proportion: $\dfrac{\text{kg}_1}{\text{cost}_1} =
\dfrac{\text{kg}_2}{\text{cost}_2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$\tfrac{5}{350} = \tfrac{8}{x}$. Cross-multiply.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x = 350 \times 8 = 2800 \Rightarrow x = 560$. **$\text{₹}560$.**
</details>

### Problem 2 — Painters and days

If $4$ painters paint a wall in $9$ days, how long will $6$ painters
take (working at the same pace)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
More painters → fewer days. *Inverse* proportion.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Painters $\times$ days $= $ constant. $4 \times 9 = 6 \times x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = \tfrac{4 \times 9}{6} = 6$ days.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Spot the regime

For each pair, decide whether the two quantities are in *direct* or
*inverse* proportion (or neither):

(a) Number of pencils bought and total cost (price per pencil fixed).
(b) Speed of a car and time to cover a fixed distance.
(c) Age of a child and their height.
(d) Number of taps filling a tank and time to fill it.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Both go up together?" → direct. "One up, one down, with a constant
product?" → inverse.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) Direct. (b) Inverse. (c) Neither (correlated, but not in a fixed
ratio or product). (d) Inverse.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Constant of proportionality

If $y$ is directly proportional to $x$, and $y = 12$ when $x = 4$,
find $y$ when $x = 11$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Direct: $\tfrac{y}{x}$ is constant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$\tfrac{12}{4} = 3$. So $y = 3x$ for any $x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = 3 \times 11 = 33$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Constant product

If $y$ is *inversely* proportional to $x$, and $y = 8$ when $x = 5$,
find $y$ when $x = 20$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Inverse: $xy$ is constant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$xy = 5 \cdot 8 = 40$. When $x = 20$, $y = \tfrac{40}{20} = 2$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Mixed problem

A car travels $480$ km in $8$ hours. (i) At the same speed, how far
does it go in $5$ hours? (ii) If the speed is doubled, how long does
it take to go $480$ km?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
(i) is direct (more time → more distance, same speed). (ii) is
inverse (more speed → less time, same distance).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Speed $= 480 / 8 = 60$ km/h. In $5$ hours: $300$ km.

**(ii)** New speed $= 120$ km/h. Time $= 480 / 120 = 4$ hours.
</details>

You have just seen — by looking at recipes, painters, taps, cars —
two ways two quantities can be linked.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

Two quantities $x$ and $y$ are in **direct proportion** if their
ratio $\tfrac{y}{x}$ is the same for every paired value. Equivalently:
$$y = kx \qquad \text{for some constant } k > 0.$$

They are in **inverse proportion** if their product $xy$ is the same
for every pair. Equivalently:
$$xy = k \quad\text{or}\quad y = \frac{k}{x} \qquad k > 0.$$

The constant $k$ is called the **constant of proportionality**.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Direct: as one rises, the other rises in step;*
> *Their ratio is fixed — multiply or divide together.*
> *Inverse: as one rises, the other must fall;*
> *Their product is fixed — when in doubt, make them swap.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> $20$ workers can build a wall in $15$ days. How many days will
> $25$ workers take?

Competent solver's path:

1. More workers → fewer days. **Inverse proportion.**
2. Constant product: workers $\times$ days $= 20 \times 15 = 300$.
3. With $25$ workers: days $= \tfrac{300}{25} = 12$.

**Answer: 12 days.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** If $7$ pens cost $\text{₹}140$, find the cost of $11$ pens.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Direct. Cost per pen $= 140/7 = \text{₹}20$. Eleven pens: $\text{₹}220$.
</details>

**W2.** If $12$ cows finish a field in $5$ days, how many days will
$15$ cows take?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Inverse. $12 \times 5 = 60 = 15 \times d \Rightarrow d = 4$ days.
</details>

**W3.** $y$ is directly proportional to $x$, and $y = 21$ when $x = 7$.
What is $y$ when $x = 13$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$k = 21/7 = 3$. $y = 3 \cdot 13 = 39$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A car uses $9$ litres of petrol for $108$ km. (i) How far on
$15$ litres? (ii) How many litres for $360$ km?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Direct. $\tfrac{\text{km}}{\text{litres}}$ is constant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$108 / 9 = 12$ km/litre.
**(i)** $15 \times 12 = 180$ km.
**(ii)** $360 / 12 = 30$ litres.
</details>

**P2.** A school feeds $250$ students with rations sufficient for $40$
days. If $50$ extra students join, how long will the same rations
last?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
More mouths → fewer days. Inverse.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$250 \times 40 = 300 \times d \Rightarrow d = 10000/300 = \tfrac{100}{3}
\approx 33.33$ days.
</details>

**P3.** $x$ and $y$ are in inverse proportion. Some paired values:

| $x$ | $4$ | $5$ | $\;?\;$ | $20$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $y$ | $30$ | $\;?\;$ | $10$ | $\;?\;$ |

Fill in the blanks.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$xy$ is constant. From $4 \times 30 = 120$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$xy = 120$.
- $x = 5 \Rightarrow y = 24$.
- $y = 10 \Rightarrow x = 12$.
- $x = 20 \Rightarrow y = 6$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** $6$ taps fill a tank in $12$ minutes. How long would $9$ taps
of the *same flow rate* take?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Inverse. $6 \times 12 = 72 = 9 \times t \Rightarrow t = 8$ minutes.
</details>

**S2.** Tea-bag manufacture: $25$ workers produce $1500$ bags in $6$
days. How many bags will $30$ workers produce in $8$ days at the same
rate?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is a *combined* proportion: bags $\propto$ workers $\times$ days.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Per-worker-per-day production: $\tfrac{1500}{25 \times 6} = 10$ bags.
Then scale.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Rate per worker per day: $\tfrac{1500}{25 \times 6} = 10$ bags.
With $30$ workers in $8$ days: $30 \times 8 \times 10 = 2400$ bags.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** If $y$ is directly proportional to $x$ and
$y = 18$ when $x = 6$, then $y$ when $x = 10$ is
(a) $30$ (b) $24$ (c) $20$ (d) $36$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$k = 3$, so $y = 30$. **Answer: (a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** A truck covers a distance of $360$ km in
$5$ hours. How long will it take to cover $450$ km at the same speed?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Direct (same speed). Time $\propto$ distance.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{360}{5} = \tfrac{450}{t} \Rightarrow t = \tfrac{450 \times 5}{360}
= \tfrac{2250}{360} = 6.25$ hours $= 6$ h $15$ min.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** $14$ pumps working $8$ hours a day empty
a tank in $6$ days. In how many days will $9$ pumps working $12$ hours
a day empty the same tank? (Assume same pump capacity.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total pump-hours is constant: pumps × hours-per-day × days = constant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$14 \times 8 \times 6 = 9 \times 12 \times d$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS $= 672$.
$d = \tfrac{672}{9 \times 12} = \tfrac{672}{108} = \tfrac{56}{9} \approx
6.22$ days.

(Often expressed as $6\tfrac{2}{9}$ days.)
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** A contractor planned to complete a road in
$30$ days with $40$ men. After $10$ days, $40\%$ of the work was done.
(i) Find the original total man-days planned. (ii) Find the man-days
already used. (iii) Find the man-days remaining and decide how many
*extra* men he must hire to finish the job in the remaining $20$ days.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Plan first, then track what's done, then size the rest.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

**(i)** Planned man-days $= 40 \times 30 = 1200$.

**(ii)** Used so far $= 40 \times 10 = 400$ man-days.

**(iii)** Work-completed should match man-days-spent in proportion *if
the plan is on track*. But $400$ man-days = $\tfrac{400}{1200} =
\tfrac{1}{3}$ of the plan, while only $40\% = \tfrac{2}{5}$ should have
been done — wait, $\tfrac{1}{3} = 33.3\%$, but $40\%$ has been done.
So progress is *ahead* of schedule by man-days spent.

Let's redo with the $40\%$ figure as ground truth:
- $400$ man-days produced $40\%$ of the work. So
  $100\%$ of the work needs $\tfrac{400}{0.4} = 1000$ man-days total.
- Already used: $400$. Remaining: $600$ man-days.
- Available time: $20$ days. Required workforce: $\tfrac{600}{20} =
  30$ men.
- Currently $40$ men are on site. **He can actually *release* $10$
  men** — no extras needed.

(The contractor's pace was faster than budgeted; the extra-men question
turns into a release-men answer. This is the kind of trap CBSE long
answers like to set.)
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 11.5 (combined work-rate
problem with men, hours, and days) is a near-twin of the 3-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2017 Set 2, Q22 ("contractor with
revised manpower") matches the 4-mark item closely.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"More always means direct proportion."** Not always. *More
   workers, fewer days* is also a "more of one" situation — but
   *inverse*. Look at whether the *product* is constant or the
   *ratio* is constant.
2. **"$x$ and $y$ correlated → $x$ and $y$ proportional."** No.
   "Older children tend to be taller" is a correlation, but it is
   neither direct nor inverse proportion.
3. **"In inverse, $\tfrac{y}{x}$ is constant."** No — *$xy$* is
   constant. The two are different.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain to a friend, without notes, the test for direct
   versus inverse proportion?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the
   7 *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a word problem, can you write the proportionality equation
   ($y = kx$ or $xy = k$) before plugging numbers?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Factorisation](../12-factorisation/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Stretch S2* and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** redo *Discover Problem 3* (regime spotting) — until you
  classify problems before solving them.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Ratio and Proportion](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)** — equality of ratios and the cross-multiplication trick.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 9 — Linear Equations in Two Variables](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd)** — direct proportion is a special case ($y = kx$).
- **[Class 10 — Trigonometry Applications](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/09-heights-and-distances/index.qmd)** — height/shadow problems hinge on direct proportion.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 11 — Direct and Inverse Proportions** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 11.1–11.3: direct
  proportion, inverse proportion, and combined / time-and-work problems.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t1.ratio-proportion`](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)
  treats ratio and proportion as foundations; this chapter formalises
  the two regimes and the constant of proportionality.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Index lookup time is *inversely proportional* to memory
spent on the index — the classic memory-vs-speed trade-off. Database
designers tune $k = $ *memory $\times$ time* to fit the workload.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Training time is *directly proportional* to the number
of training examples (with the network fixed) — a fact every ML
engineer rediscovers when their dataset doubles.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The first rule of physics — Newton's third law,
Boyle's law ($PV = $ constant), Coulomb's law ($\propto 1/r^2$) — is
*always* a proportionality. Recognising the regime is the first step
in every modelling exercise.
:::
